evidence: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations used at 82 sites across the codebase — on method parameters, return types, and record fields (e.g. RelationConfig components, ExplorationResult fields, HttpApiUtils method signatures).
Sealed interface QueryResults.ConcordanceResult with 'permits QueryResults.SnippetResult, QueryResults.CollocateResult' — correct use of algebraic sum types.

Records used consistently for immutable value objects: CollocateProfile, CoreCollocate, Edge, FetchExamplesOptions, ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, RelationConfig, DiscoveredNoun, ComparisonResult.SummaryCounts.
Enums (RelationEdgeType, SharingCategory, PosGroup) carry separate stable string labels distinct from their Java constant names, preventing API-consumer coupling to Java identifier conventions.
Optional used appropriately as return types: GrammarConfig.relation(), RelationConfig.relationType(), CollocateProfile.strongestNoun() — not as field storage in most places.

Pervasive Map<String, Object> for HTTP response construction across all API handlers (56 occurrences in api/ package). Field names are stringly-typed string literals; no typed response DTOs or records exist at the API boundary.

RelationConfig record contains 'Optional<RelationType> relationType' as a record component — using Optional as a stored field value rather than a return type is a documented Java anti-pattern (Optional is not Serializable and is intended for use as a return type only).

CollocateQueryHelper has a test-only no-arg constructor that sets 'this.index = null' while the field is declared as 'private final BlackLabIndex index' without @Nullable annotation — the field type does not reflect the nullable production invariant.

RelationType enum contains four legacy semantic-role constants (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) documented as 'not set in grammar config' — the enum type's precision is diluted by dead constants that live alongside the active SURFACE/DEP dispatch values.

ExplorationResult passes Map<String, Double> and Map<String, Map<String, Double>> as constructor arguments without defensive copies for seedCollocates/perSeedCollocates, so external mutation of the maps passed in would corrupt the 'immutable' result object.

strengths: Consistent, broad use of JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations (82 sites) signals a genuine commitment to nullability contracts at public boundaries.
Sealed interface QueryResults.ConcordanceResult with explicit permits clause is a textbook algebraic sum type — compiler-enforced exhaustiveness rather than ad-hoc instanceof chains.
Records adopted uniformly for all immutable value objects in model.exploration, model, and config packages, leveraging structural typing and automatic equals/hashCode.
Enums carry stable API-safe labels separate from Java constant names, preventing serialization coupling and enabling safe renaming.
Optional used exclusively as return types, not as parameter types, following the intended design of java.util.Optional.
type_discipline.issue_character: The codebase has strong type discipline in its domain/model layer but systematically abandons it at the API boundary, where all HTTP response objects are assembled as untyped Map<String, Object> with stringly-typed field names rather than typed response records; secondary issues are using Optional as a record component field and a nullable field undeclared as @Nullable.
type_discipline.score_rationale: The model and config layers score near the top of the range — sealed types, records, enums with labels, and pervasive nullability annotations represent deliberate, well-executed type discipline. The score is pulled down materially by the API layer's systematic reliance on Map<String, Object> for response construction (56 occurrences), which erases all compile-time guarantees for the outbound API shape and creates a stringly-typed surface across the entire HTTP boundary. This pattern is idiomatic in simple Java HTTP handlers but, given the codebase's otherwise high type discipline, represents a gap that would score around 45-50 in isolation, landing the overall dimension at 72.

issues.summary: All HTTP response bodies assembled as Map<String, Object> with stringly-typed field names — no compile-time verification of response shape
evidence: ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison builds Map<String, Object> with 'response.put("status", "ok")' and 'response.put("seeds", ...)' — field names are untyped string literals.
ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse returns void and mutates a passed Map<String, Object> — response shape is invisible to the compiler.
56 Map<String, Object> occurrences counted in the api/ package alone, all for response construction.
issues.suggestion: Introduce typed response record classes (e.g., ExploreResponse, ComparisonResponse, SketchResponse) in a model.api or api.response package and serialize those with fastjson2 instead of ad-hoc maps. Records are directly serializable by fastjson2; this gives compile-time field verification, enables IDE rename refactoring, and self-documents the public API shape.

issues.summary: Optional<RelationType> used as a record component field in RelationConfig — Optional is designed as a return type, not a field storage type
evidence: RelationConfig record declares 'Optional<RelationType> relationType' as a component — Optional does not implement Serializable and is explicitly not intended for field storage per its JavaDoc.
Callsites use 'rel.relationType().orElse(null)' and 'rel.relationType().isEmpty()' — treating Optional as a field defeats its readability benefit compared to a @Nullable RelationType field.
issues.suggestion: Replace 'Optional<RelationType> relationType' with '@Nullable RelationType relationType' in the record; change callers to null-check directly or use Optional.ofNullable(rel.relationType()) at the call site. This aligns with the codebase's existing JSpecify annotation strategy.

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.index is assigned null in the test constructor but the field lacks @Nullable annotation
evidence: Field declared as 'private final BlackLabIndex index' with no nullability annotation.
Test-only no-arg constructor sets 'this.index = null;' — violates the @NonNull contract that the production constructor enforces via @NonNull parameter annotation.
Runtime guards (Objects.requireNonNull) are added inside methods to detect null — these are compensating for the missing type annotation.
issues.suggestion: Annotate the field as '@Nullable BlackLabIndex index' and add a Javadoc note that null is only valid for test subclasses. Alternatively, if only tests use the no-arg constructor, introduce a protected overrideable method for each I/O operation so test subclasses override behaviour rather than nulling out the field.

root_causes: The API layer was likely built incrementally using the idiomatic Java-HTTP-handler pattern (Map + put) without later adopting typed response DTOs as the model layer matured.
Optional as a record field is a common mistake introduced when converting a class with an Optional-returning getter to a record, where the getter body becomes the field itself.
